text: Module -1 ARTIFICIAL NEURAL NETWORK •An ANN is an information processing system that mimic the working of human brain. •It consists of collection of artificial neurons(mathematical model of neurons)arranged in layers DEPT OF AD,SJCET,PALAI 3 DEPT OF AD,SJCET,PALAI 4 Neurons are the building blocks of the nervous system. They receive and transmit signals to different parts of the body The neurons are connected to one another with the use of axons and dendrites , and the connecting regions between axons and dendrites are referred to as synapses . •All neurons have three different parts – dendrites, cell body and axon. ❑ Dendrites •These are branch -like structures that receive messages from other •neurons and allow the transmission of messages to the cell body. ❑ Cell Body •Each neuron has a cell body with a nucleus. It is the cell body of the neuron and is responsible for processing of information, they have received from dendrites. DEPT OF AD,SJCET,PALAI 5 ❑Axon •Axon is a tube -like structure that carries electrical impulse from the cell body to the axon terminals that pass the impulse to another neuron. ❑Synapse •It is the chemical junction between the terminal of one neuron and the dendrites of another neuron. DEPT OF AD,SJCET,PALAI 6 Models of Neuron Logic circuits realized using MP Neuron •In the MP model the weights are fixed. •Hence a network using this model does not have the capability of learning McCulloch and Pitts Neuron McCulloch and Pitts Neuron ►The first Mathematical /computational model of a Biological Neuron was proposed by Warren MuCulloch (neuroscientist) and Walter Pitts (logician) in 1943. ►It is considered as the earliest Neural Network and is also called M-P Neuron . ►M P Neurons are connected by directed weighted paths. ►It’s main purpose is to process the binary data .i-e the activation of a M -P neuron is binary(at any time neuron may fire/may not fire). ►Logic gates /functions can be used in application ►It may be divided into 2 parts. The first part, gtakes an input , performs an aggregation and based on the aggregated value the second part, fmakes a decision . Perceptron Model •There is a perceptron learning law which gives a step -by-step procedure for adjusting the weights. •Whether the weight adjustment converges or not depends on the nature of the desired input -output pairs to be represented by the model. •The perceptron convergence theorem enables us to determine whether the given pattern pairs are representable or not. • If the weight values converge, then the corresponding problem is said to be represented by the perceptron network Adaline •ADAptive LINear Element (ADALINE) is a computing model proposed by Widrow and is shown. TOPOLOGY •ANNs are effective when processing units are organized properly. •The arrangement of units, connections, and input/output is called topology . •Different architectures support different pattern recognition tasks. •Layers in ANNs •ANNs are organized into layers of processing units. •Each layer's units have the same activation dynamics and output function. •Connections can be: •Interlayer (between layers) •Intralayer (within the same layer) •Both interlayer and intralayer •Types of Network Connections •Feedforward: Signals move in one direction. •Feedback: Signals can loop back to previous units. •Feedback networks allow repeated unit activation. •Basic Network Structures •Instar & Outstar : •Instar: Each unit in F2 receives inputs from all units in F1. •Outstar: Each unit in F1 connects to multiple units in F2. •Heteroassociation Networks : •Group of instars (F1 → F2) •Group of outstars (F2 → F1) •Bidirectional Associative Memory (BAM) : •Two-way flow between layers (F1 F2) •Instar Learning •Instar adjusts weights to match input vector.

(between layers) •Intralayer (within the same layer) •Both interlayer and intralayer •Types of Network Connections •Feedforward: Signals move in one direction. •Feedback: Signals can loop back to previous units. •Feedback networks allow repeated unit activation. •Basic Network Structures •Instar & Outstar : •Instar: Each unit in F2 receives inputs from all units in F1. •Outstar: Each unit in F1 connects to multiple units in F2. •Heteroassociation Networks : •Group of instars (F1 → F2) •Group of outstars (F2 → F1) •Bidirectional Associative Memory (BAM) : •Two-way flow between layers (F1 F2) •Instar Learning •Instar adjusts weights to match input vector. •Activation of a unit in F2 reaches maximum when input a is applied to F1. •Instar behaves like content -addressable memory. •Outstar Learning •Weights adjust based on activity pattern in F1. •When unit j in F2 is activated, it reproduces the original input pattern. •Outstar behaves like memory addressing contents. •Autoassociative Memory •When F1 and F2 coincide and weights are symmetric ( wji = wij). •Each unit is connected to every other unit. •Functions as a self -organizing memory. Topology •The arrangement of the processing units, connections, and pattern input output is referred to as topology. •INSTAR •An instar is a fan -in neural network structure where all units in the input layer are connected to a single unit in the output layer. •During learning, the weight vector of the output neuron is gradually adjusted to match the input pattern presented at the input layer. •When the weight vector becomes aligned with the input vector, the neuron produces maximum activation. •Hence, instar networks function as content -addressable memory systems used for pattern recognition and classification. INSTAR •Definition: Instar is a neural structure where connections are directed from all units in one layer ( 𝐹1 ) to a single unit in another layer ( 𝐹2​ ). • This results in a fan -in geometry •Learning Rule:During learning, the normalized weight vector 𝑤𝑗=(𝑤𝑗1,𝑤𝑗2,...,𝑤𝑗𝑀) associated with the j -th unit in 𝐹2 adapts to match the input vector 𝑎=(𝑎1,𝑎2,...,𝑎𝑀) presented at 𝐹1​ . •The j -th unit is activated to its maximum extent when w j​ becomes aligned with 𝑎 •Purpose: Instar networks perform content -addressable memory, which allows the network to recognize or classify patterns by associating input vectors to specific memory units. OUTSTAR •Definition: Outstar is a neural structure where connections are directed from a single unit in one layer ( 𝐹2) to all units in another layer ( 𝐹1​ ). •This results in a fan -out geometry. •Learning Rule:During learning, the weight vector for connections from the 𝑗-th unit in 𝐹2​ to 𝐹1​ becomes aligned with the activity pattern in 𝐹1 when an input vector 𝑎 is presented at 𝐹1. •Recall Phase:If the 𝑗-th unit in 𝐹2 is activated, the weight vector from that unit transmits a signal pattern to 𝐹1​ . •This process reconstructs the original activity pattern (corresponding to the input vector 𝑎) in 𝐹1 , even when 𝑎 is absent. •Purpose: Outstar networks perform memory addressing, enabling the network to retrieve content based on a memory address. •An outstar is a fan -out neural network structure in which a single unit in one layer is connected to all units in another layer. •During learning, the outgoing weight vector of the active neuron becomes aligned with the activity pattern in the output layer. •During recall, activation of the neuron causes the stored weight pattern to be transmitted, reconstructing the original activity pattern even in the absence of the input. •Hence, outstar networks perform memory addressing. NETWORK ARCHITECTURES •The manner in which the neurons of a neural network are

the network to retrieve content based on a memory address. •An outstar is a fan -out neural network structure in which a single unit in one layer is connected to all units in another layer. •During learning, the outgoing weight vector of the active neuron becomes aligned with the activity pattern in the output layer. •During recall, activation of the neuron causes the stored weight pattern to be transmitted, reconstructing the original activity pattern even in the absence of the input. •Hence, outstar networks perform memory addressing. NETWORK ARCHITECTURES •The manner in which the neurons of a neural network are structured is intimately linked with the learning algorithm used to train the network. •We may therefore speak of learning algorithms (rules) used in the design of neural networks as being structured. •Three fundamentally different classes of network architectures: 1. Single layer –Feedforward Networks •Neurons in a layered neural network are arranged in distinct layers •The simplest layered network consists of: •An input layer (source nodes) •An output layer (computation neurons) •The input layer projects directly to the output layer •There are no backward connections from output to input •Hence, the network is strictly feedforward •The term single -layer refers only to the output (computational) layer •The input layer is not counted since no computation occurs there Multilayer feedforward Networks •A feedforward neural network with one or more hidden layers forms the second class of feedforward networks. •The neurons in these layers are called hidden neurons (hidden units) . •The term hidden means these layers are not directly visible from the network’s input or output. •Hidden neurons mediate between input and output , transforming input signals into useful internal representations. •Adding hidden layers allows the network to extract higher -order statistical features from the input data. •The input layer (source nodes) supplies elements of the input vector (activation pattern). •These inputs are fed into the first hidden layer , where computation takes place. •The output of each layer serves as the input to the next layer . •Typically, neurons in a given layer receive inputs only from the immediately preceding layer . •The final output layer produces the overall response of the network to the input pattern . Recurrent neural networks •A recurrent neural network (RNN) differs from a feedforward neural network by having at least one feedback loop . •In an RNN, the output of neurons is fed back into the network as input. •An example RNN may consist of a single layer of neurons , where each neuron feeds its output to the inputs of other neurons In the illustrated structure (Fig. 17): •There are no self -feedback loops . •Self -feedback means a neuron’s output is fed back into its own input . •The network contains no hidden neurons . •Another class of recurrent networks (Fig. 18) includes hidden neurons . •In these networks, feedback connections originate from both hidden neurons and output neurons . •The presence of feedback loops: •Significantly affects the learning capability of the network. •Has a strong impact on overall network performance . •Feedback loops use unit-time delay elements (denoted by z−1z^{ -1}z−1). •These delay elements introduce nonlinear dynamic behavior , provided the network contains nonlinear units. Knowledge Representation •Knowledge refers to stored information or models used by a person or machine to interpret, predict, and appropriately respond to the outside world. •The primary characteristics of knowledge representation are twofold: 1. What information is made explicit. 2.How the information is physically encoded for subsequent use. Goal -Directed Nature •By its very nature, knowledge representation is goal directed. •A good solution depends on a good

•Has a strong impact on overall network performance . •Feedback loops use unit-time delay elements (denoted by z−1z^{ -1}z−1). •These delay elements introduce nonlinear dynamic behavior , provided the network contains nonlinear units. Knowledge Representation •Knowledge refers to stored information or models used by a person or machine to interpret, predict, and appropriately respond to the outside world. •The primary characteristics of knowledge representation are twofold: 1. What information is made explicit. 2.How the information is physically encoded for subsequent use. Goal -Directed Nature •By its very nature, knowledge representation is goal directed. •A good solution depends on a good representation of knowledge. Knowledge of the World •A major task for a neural network is to learn a model of the world (environment) in which it is embedded, and to maintain the model sufficiently consistently with the real world so as to achieve the specified goals of the application of interest. •Knowledge of the world consists of two kinds of information: •The known world state, represented by facts about what is and what has been known; this form of knowledge is referred to as prior information . •Observations (measurements) of the world, obtained by means of sensors designed to probe the environment, in which the neural network is supposed to operate. •Observations obtained from the environment are inherently noisy. •Observations form a pool of information •Training examples are drawn from this pool Examples •Prior information:Digits are known to belong to classes 0 –9, and pixels form structured patterns. •Observations:Images of handwritten digits collected using scanners or cameras. Training Examples •A set of examples, labeled or unlabeled d. •These examples represent knowledge about the environment. •In labeled examples, each input signal is paired with a corresponding desired response (target output). •Used in supervised learning. •Unlabeled examples consist only of input signals. •No desired response is provided. •Used in unsupervised learning Example: Handwritten Digit Recognition •Input: Image of handwritten digit (black and white pixels). •Output: Identity of the digit (0 –9). •Training data contains many digit samples. Knowledge Stored in Network •In a neural network of specified architecture, knowledge representation of the surrounding environment is defined by the values taken by synaptic weights and biases. •The form of knowledge representation constitutes the very design of the neural network and therefore, holds the key to its performance. Roles of Knowledge Representation •The subject of how knowledge is actually represented inside an artificial network is, however, very complicated. •Nevertheless, there are four rules for knowledge representation that are of a general commonsense nature. Rule 1: Similar Inputs •Similar inputs (i.e., patterns drawn) from similar classes should usually produce similar representations inside the network, and should therefore be classified as belonging to the same class. •Rule 2: Separate Classes •Items to be categorized as separate classes should be given widely different representations in the network. Rule 3: Important Features •If a particular feature is important, then there should be a large number of neurons involved in the representation of that item in the network. •Rule 4: Prior Information and Invariances •Prior information and invariances should be built into the design of a neural network whenever they are available, so as to simplify the network design by its not having to learn them. Learning Process (p.34) •Learning = weight adaptation •Based on experience •Uses learning algorithms •Learning Paradigm •Input from environment •Output from network •Error computation •Weight update Learning Algorithm •Defines how weights change •Objective: minimize error •Iterative process •Types of Learning •Supervised learning •Unsupervised learning •Reinforcement learning Supervised Learning •Uses labeled data •Error = desired –actual output •Examples: Perceptron, Backpropagation •No target output •Discovers structure

4: Prior Information and Invariances •Prior information and invariances should be built into the design of a neural network whenever they are available, so as to simplify the network design by its not having to learn them. Learning Process (p.34) •Learning = weight adaptation •Based on experience •Uses learning algorithms •Learning Paradigm •Input from environment •Output from network •Error computation •Weight update Learning Algorithm •Defines how weights change •Objective: minimize error •Iterative process •Types of Learning •Supervised learning •Unsupervised learning •Reinforcement learning Supervised Learning •Uses labeled data •Error = desired –actual output •Examples: Perceptron, Backpropagation •No target output •Discovers structure •Examples: SOM, PCAUnsupervised Learning Reinforcement Learning •• Learning via reward/penalty •• No explicit teacher •• Agent –environment interaction Learning Tasks (p.38) •Pattern classification •Function approximation •Prediction and control Generalization •• Ability to handle unseen data •• Key goal of learning •• Avoid overfitting Stability–Plasticity Dilemma •• Stability: retain old knowledge •• Plasticity: learn new info •• Balance is essential Categories of learning •Hebbian learning •Perceptron Learning Rule •Delta Learning Rule •Generalized Delta Learning Rule •Competitive learning •Errorcorrection learning •Reinforcement learning, Introduction •The generalized delta learning law is an extension of the delta rule. •It is used for training multilayer feedforward neural networks. •It forms the basis of the backpropagation algorithm. Need for Generalized Delta Rule •Simple delta rule works only for single -layer networks. •Multilayer networks contain hidden layers without target outputs. •Hence, error must be propagated backward. Network Structure •Multilayer feedforward network consists of: •-Input layer •-Hidden layer(s) •-Output layer •Learning is supervised. Error Criterion •Objective is to minimize mean square error (MSE). •E = 1/2 Σ (d -y)² •Gradient descent is used for minimization. Generalized Delta Learning Rule •Weight update rule: •Δwᵢⱼ= η δ ⱼyᵢ •η –learning rate •δⱼ–local error signal Error Signal: Output Layer •For output neuron j: •δⱼ= (dⱼ− yⱼ) f′(v ⱼ) •Depends on output error and activation derivative. Error Signal: Hidden Layer •For hidden neuron j: •δⱼ= f′(v ⱼ) Σ δₖ w ⱼₖ •Error is propagated backward from output layer. Learning Algorithm Steps •1. Initialize weights randomly •2. Forward propagate inputs •3. Compute output error •4. Backpropagate error •5. Update weights •6. Repeat until convergence Conclusion •Generalized delta learning law enables learning in multilayer networks. •It overcomes limitations of single -layer delta rule. •Forms the foundation of backpropagation learning. Competitive Learning •Competitive learning refers to a class of learning laws in which neurons compete with each other to respond to an input pattern. •Only the neuron (or a small set of neurons) that wins the competition is allowed to adapt its synaptic weights . •This type of learning is generally unsupervised . •Unlike Hebbian learning, where multiple output neurons can be active, only one neuron is active at a time in competitive learning. •This is known as the winner -takes -all strategy •Basic Elements of Competitive Learning Rule •A set of identical neurons (same structure, different synaptic weights). •A limit on the strength of each neuron (to prevent unlimited growth of weights). •A competition mechanism among neurons so that only one neuron wins and updates its weights. •Learning laws which modulate the difference between the output signal and the synaptic weight belong to the category of competitive learning. The general form of competitive learning is given by the following expression for the synaptic dynamics [Grossberg, 19691: This is also called the deterministic competitive learning law. It can be written as •Here learning or adjustment of weights takes place only when there is a nonzero post -synaptic signal ( si).If si = 0, then the synaptic weights do not change. It is also interesting to note

among neurons so that only one neuron wins and updates its weights. •Learning laws which modulate the difference between the output signal and the synaptic weight belong to the category of competitive learning. The general form of competitive learning is given by the following expression for the synaptic dynamics [Grossberg, 19691: This is also called the deterministic competitive learning law. It can be written as •Here learning or adjustment of weights takes place only when there is a nonzero post -synaptic signal ( si).If si = 0, then the synaptic weights do not change. It is also interesting to note that, unlike in the Hebbian case, in the competitive learning case the system does not forget the past learning when the post -synaptic signal is zero. •The competitive learning works in a situation where an external input is presented to an input layer of units and these units feed signals to each of the units in the output layer. •The signals from the units in the output layer compete with each other, leaving eventually one of the units (say i) as the winner. •The weights leading to this unit are adjusted according to the learning law. This is also called the 'winner take -all' situation, since only one unit in the output layer will have a nonzero output eventually. •The corresponding weights wij from all the input units ( j )are adjusted to match the input vector. •While the Hebbian learning is generally distributed, i.e., all the weights are adjusted for every input pattern, the competitive learning is not distributed. •In fact, the input vectors leading to the same winning unit in the competitive layer will produce a weight vector for that unit which is an average of all the corresponding input vector. ERROR CORRECTION LEARNING Error signal •Error signal is defined as: •eₖ(n) = dₖ(n) − yₖ(n) •dₖ(n): desired output •yₖ(n): actual output •- Measures difference between desired and actual output •- Activates learning control mechanism •- Guides weight adjustment Weight Update Rule •Δwₖⱼ(n) = η eₖ(n) x ⱼ(n) •η: learning rate •xⱼ(n): input signal •Error magnitude controls size of update •Learning rate controls speed and stability •Weight update is input dependent Stability and Convergence •Stability refers to the equilibrium behavior of a neural network's activation state. •Convergence refers to the adjustment of weights during learning, minimizing error. •Both concepts are crucial in supervised and unsupervised learning. •The objective of any learning law is that it should eventually lead to a set of weights which will capture the pattern information in the training set data. Stability in Neural Networks •In general, the activation state of the network starts from an initial state and follows a trajectory dictated by the dynamics of the equations. •A network will be useful only if a trajectory leads eventually to an equilibrium state at which point there is no further change in the state. Such a state is also called a stable state, when a small perturbation of the state settles to the same state. •Different initial states may follow different trajectories, all of which should terminate at some equilibrium states. •There may be several trajectories that may terminate at the same equilibrium state. •Equilibrium states enable pattern storage in networks. •The global pattern thus formed will only change if there is a different external input Types of Stability •Fixed Point Stability : The network settles to a stable state. •Such a network then can form global patterns at those states, and hence can be used for pattern storage. •One of the conditions is that the weights {cik)should be symmetric •Oscillatory Stability : The

state. •Different initial states may follow different trajectories, all of which should terminate at some equilibrium states. •There may be several trajectories that may terminate at the same equilibrium state. •Equilibrium states enable pattern storage in networks. •The global pattern thus formed will only change if there is a different external input Types of Stability •Fixed Point Stability : The network settles to a stable state. •Such a network then can form global patterns at those states, and hence can be used for pattern storage. •One of the conditions is that the weights {cik)should be symmetric •Oscillatory Stability : The network exhibits periodic oscillations but remains stable. •These oscillatory regions are also stable, and hence can be used for pattern storage. •Oscillatory stable states may also arise when there is some delay in the feedback of the outputs from other processing units to the current unit, even though the weights are exactly symmetric •Chaotic Stability : The network undergoes chaotic but bounded changes. Convergence in Learning •Learning aims to adjust weights so the network captures patterns. •Weight updates should minimize the error between actual and desired outputs. •Convergence is a key goal in neural network training •Stability ensures reliable pattern storage and retrieval. •Convergence ensures effective learning by minimizing errors. •Understanding these principles is essential for designing efficient neural networks. Role of Lyapunov Function •A Lyapunov function (V(x)) helps analyze stability. •It acts as an energy function, ensuring trajectories move towards equilibrium. •If V(x) decreases over time, the system is stable. •Stability Theorems •Cohen -Grossberg Theorem: Ensures stability in fixed -weight autoassociative networks. •Cohen -Grossberg -Kosko Theorem: Applies to adaptive autoassociative networks. •Adaptive Bidirectional Associative Memory Theorem: Deals with heteroassociative networks. Reinforcement learning •Supervised Learning (Learning with Teacher): Desired output is known, learning is based on error correction. •Reinforcement Learning (Learning with Critic): Desired output is unknown, feedback is evaluative. •Output is evaluated rather than corrected. •Uses a reinforcement signal (reward or penalty). •Example: Learning in a game where success is determined at the end. Feedback from Environment •The environment provides reinforcement signals. •Three types of reinforcement learning: •Fixed Credit Assignment: Fixed reinforcement signals. •Probabilistic Credit Assignment: Stochastic reinforcement based on probability. •Temporal Credit Assignment: Reinforcement depends on past outputs. Associative Reward -Penalty Learning •Proposed by Barto and Anandan (1985). •Uses learning rate parameters: •Positive Reinforcement (Reward): High learning rate. •Negative Reinforcement (Penalty): Lower learning rate. •Formula involves expected output and learning rate. •Single -layer networks use basic reinforcement learning rules. •Multi -layer networks require backpropagation of error to adjust hidden layers. Hebb Network ►In 1949, Donald Hebb proposed one of the key ideas in biological learning, commonly known as Hebb’s Law. ►Hebb introduced a neurophysiological postulate : “…When an axon of cell A is near enough to excite a cell B and repeatedly and persistently takes part in firing it, some growth process or metabolic change takes place in one or both cells, such that A’s efficiency as one of the cells firing B, is increased.” ►Hebb’s Law states that if neuron i is near enough to excite neuron j and repeatedly participates in its activation, the synaptic connection between these two neurons is strengthened and neuron j becomes more sensitive to stimuli from neuron i Hebb Network ►basically the above explanation is derived from the modus operandi (the way in which something operates or works)used by the brain where learning is performed by the changes in the synaptic gap ►In this, if 2 interconnected neurons are ON simultaneously then the weight associated with these neurons can be increased by the modification made in their synaptic gaps(strength). ►Hebb’s Law

that if neuron i is near enough to excite neuron j and repeatedly participates in its activation, the synaptic connection between these two neurons is strengthened and neuron j becomes more sensitive to stimuli from neuron i Hebb Network ►basically the above explanation is derived from the modus operandi (the way in which something operates or works)used by the brain where learning is performed by the changes in the synaptic gap ►In this, if 2 interconnected neurons are ON simultaneously then the weight associated with these neurons can be increased by the modification made in their synaptic gaps(strength). ►Hebb’s Law can be represented in the form of two rules: ►If two neurons on either side of a connection are activated synchronously , then the weight of that connection is increased . ►If two neurons on either side of a connection are activated asynchronously , then the weight of that connection is decreased . ► Hebb’s Law provides the basis for learning without a teacher . ► Learning here is a local phenomenon occurring without feedback from the environment. ►It is a kind of feed -forward, unsupervised learning . Page 108 Adaptive Linear Neuron (Adaline) ►Theunits with linear activation function arecalled linear units. ►Anetwork with asingle linear unitiscalled anAdaline (adaptive linear neuron). ►InanAdaline, theinput -output relationship islinear . ►Adaline uses bipolar activation foritsinput signals anditstarget output. ►Theweights between theinput andtheoutput areadjustable. ►ThebiasinAdaline actslikeanadjustable weight, whose connection isfrom aunit with activations being always 1. ►Adaline isanetwhich hasonly oneoutput unit.
